disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Acute escalation of repetitive head-banging and self-biting over about three hours, beginning shortly after an unexpected change to the evening meal routine
trigger_question_examples: Can you tell me exactly what he is doing and when it started?
Did anything change or happen just before this began?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: It started about three hours ago right after his dinner routine got changed. He began with hand-wringing and loud noises and now he's banging his head over and over and biting himself.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Physical aggression toward caregivers — biting and lunging at anyone who approaches
trigger_question_examples: Is he a danger to himself or to the staff right now?
Can anyone safely get near him?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He's biting and lunging at anyone who comes close. None of us can safely get near him at the moment.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Visible lump and fresh grazes on the forehead from repeated head impacts
trigger_question_examples: Has he injured himself — can you see any marks or swelling?
Where on his head is he hitting himself?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: Yes — there's a big lump and some fresh grazes on the front of his head where he keeps hitting it against the window.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Patient has profound autism spectrum disorder and is non-verbal, unable to communicate pain or basic needs
trigger_question_examples: Can he tell you what's wrong or where it hurts?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: He can't talk at all and can't tell us if something hurts — he has profound autism and communicates only through sounds and behaviour.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Standard de-escalation and the as-needed oral medication have not worked; staff cannot safely give the tablet because he is biting
trigger_question_examples: Has he been able to take any of his calming medication?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: We've tried our usual quiet-space and distraction approach and it isn't working. We have his as-needed tablet but we can't get him to take it safely because he's biting.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Environmental hazards present — broken glass on the floor and doors locked because of a high risk of him running off
trigger_question_examples: Is the area around him safe?
Is there anything he could hurt himself on?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: There's broken glass on the floor from a smashed picture frame, and we've had to lock the doors because he tries to run off.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: Recent changes that could indicate a hidden painful cause — bowel habit, eating and drinking, ear or tooth trouble, or urinary changes — cannot be confirmed because the patient cannot report them
trigger_question_examples: Has anything changed recently with his bowels, waterworks, eating, or any sign of ear or tooth pain?
Could something be hurting him that he can't tell you about?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: We're honestly not sure — he can't tell us. Now that you ask, we don't think he's had his bowels open for a couple of days, but we can't be certain.
disclosure_items.patient_deflection_template: We just assumed it was his autism and the routine change — we hadn't thought about him being in pain.

disclosure_items.clinical_fact: No vomiting, no unequal pupils, and he is awake and alert (though highly agitated) at present
trigger_question_examples: Has he vomited, become drowsy, or do his pupils look uneven?
disclosure_items.patient_response_template: No vomiting and he's very much awake — if anything too awake. His pupils look normal to us but we can't get close.
